stem: Let \( F \) be the finite field with \( [[qval]] = [[p]]^{[[k]]} \) elements. Its nonzero elements form a group under multiplication. What is the order of this multiplicative group \( F^{\times} \)?
solution: The multiplicative group \( F^{\times} \) consists of all nonzero elements of \( F \). Since \( F \) has \( [[qval]] \) elements, exactly one of which is \( 0 \), the order of \( F^{\times} \) is \( [[qval]] - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the order of the whole field \( [[qval]] \); the multiplicative group excludes \( 0 \).
This subtracts two, excluding both \( 0 \) and \( 1 \); but \( 1 \) is the identity and belongs to \( F^{\times} \).
This computes \( p \cdot k - 1 \), using \( p \cdot k \) instead of \( p^{k} \) for the field size.
This swaps base and exponent, using \( k^{p} - 1 \) instead of \( p^{k} - 1 \).
This is two more than the correct order.
This is three more than the correct order.

stem: Let \( F = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) be the finite field with \( [[p]] \) elements. Its multiplicative group \( F^{\times} \) is cyclic of order \( [[p]]-1 \). How many of the \( [[p]]-1 \) nonzero elements are generators of \( F^{\times} \) (that is, have multiplicative order exactly \( [[p]]-1 \))?
solution: A cyclic group of order \( n \) has exactly \( \varphi(n) \) generators. Here \( [[p]] \) is a safe prime, \( [[p]] - 1 = 2r \) with \( r = \dfrac{[[p]]-1}{2} \) an odd prime, so \( \varphi([[p]]-1) = \varphi(2)\varphi(r) = 1\cdot(r-1) = \dfrac{[[p]]-1}{2} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the full order of \( F^{\times} \); not every nonzero element is a generator, only the primitive ones.
This is one less than the group order, not the count of generators.
This is \( r = \frac{p-1}{2} \) itself, i.e. \( \varphi(2)\varphi(r) \) computed as \( r \) instead of \( r - 1 \) (forgetting \( \varphi(r) = r-1 \), not \( r \)).
This is three less than the group order, an arbitrary offset rather than the totient count.
This is one more than the correct count of generators.
This is one fewer than the correct count of generators.

stem: Let \( F \) be a finite field with \( [[qval]] \) elements, so its multiplicative group \( F^{\times} \) is cyclic of order \( [[qm1]] \). Given that \( [[d]] \) divides \( [[qm1]] \), how many elements \( x \in F^{\times} \) satisfy \( x^{[[d]]} = 1 \)?
solution: A cyclic group of order \( n \) has, for each divisor \( d \mid n \), a UNIQUE subgroup of order \( d \), and that subgroup is exactly the set of solutions to \( x^{d}=1 \). So the number of solutions is \( d = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the order of the whole group \( F^{\times} \); by Lagrange every element satisfies \( x^{[[qm1]]}=1 \), but far fewer satisfy the smaller exponent \( [[d]] \).
This is the index \( [[qm1]]/[[d]] \) of the subgroup, not its order.
This is one more than the number of solutions.
This is one fewer than the number of solutions.
This doubles \( d \) instead of using it directly as the subgroup order.
This is the size of the complement, not of the solution set itself.

stem: Let \( F \) be a finite field with \( [[qval]] \) elements, so \( F^{\times} \) is cyclic of order \( [[qm1]] \). Since \( [[dval]] = [[r]] \cdot [[s]] \) (a product of the distinct primes \( [[r]] \) and \( [[s]] \)) divides \( [[qm1]] \), how many elements of \( F^{\times} \) have multiplicative order EXACTLY \( [[dval]] \)?
solution: In a cyclic group of order \( n \), the number of elements of order exactly \( d \mid n \) is \( \varphi(d) \). Here \( d = [[dval]] = [[r]]\cdot[[s]] \) with \( [[r]], [[s]] \) distinct primes, so \( \varphi(d) = \varphi([[r]])\varphi([[s]]) = ([[r]]-1)([[s]]-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts elements with \( x^{[[dval]]}=1 \) (order DIVIDING \( d \)), not elements of order EXACTLY \( d \).
This is one less than \( d \), not the totient of \( d \).
This adds \( r+s-2 \) instead of multiplying \( (r-1)(s-1) \); \( \varphi \) is multiplicative, not additive, on coprime factors.
This is the index \( [[qm1]]/d \) of the order-\( d \) subgroup, not the count of order-exactly-\( d \) elements within it.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one fewer than the correct count.

stem: Let \( F \) be a finite field with \( [[qval]] \) elements and let \( g \) be a generator of the cyclic group \( F^{\times} \), which has order \( [[qm1]] \). What is the multiplicative order of \( g^{[[e]]} \)?
solution: In a cyclic group of order \( n \), the element \( g^{e} \) has order \( \dfrac{n}{\gcd(n,e)} \). Here \( n = [[qm1]] \) and \( e = [[e]] \), so the order of \( g^{[[e]]} \) is \( \dfrac{[[qm1]]}{\gcd([[qm1]],[[e]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the order of \( g \) itself; raising \( g \) to a power \( e>1 \) generally shrinks the order unless \( e \) is coprime to \( [[qm1]] \).
This treats the exponent \( e \) itself as the order, ignoring the group's actual order \( [[qm1]] \) entirely.
This reports \( \gcd([[qm1]],[[e]]) \), the reciprocal factor, instead of \( [[qm1]]/\gcd([[qm1]],[[e]]) \).
This subtracts \( e \) from \( [[qm1]] \) instead of dividing by the gcd.
This is one more than the correct order.
This is one fewer than the correct order.
